content.title: Currency subdivision: 分 (fēn)
content.body:
分 (fēn) is 1/100 of a yuan (1 cent):

- 一分 (yī fēn) = 0.01 yuan
- 五分 (wǔ fēn) = 0.05 yuan

分 is rarely used today because prices are usually in whole yuan or jiǎo, but it's still part of the currency system.

content.title: Currency subdivision: 角 (jiǎo) and 毛 (máo)
content.body:
角 (jiǎo) is 1/10 of a yuan (10 cents). In spoken Chinese, it's called 毛 (máo):

- 一角 (yī jiǎo) = 0.1 yuan (formal)
- 一毛 (yī máo) = 0.1 yuan (spoken)
- 五角 (wǔ jiǎo) = 0.5 yuan
- 九毛 (jiǔ máo) = 0.9 yuan

content.title: Numbers 1,000-9,999 with 千 (qiān)
content.body:
千 (qiān) means "thousand." Place a digit (1-9) before 千:

- 一千 (yī qiān) = 1,000
- 二千 (èr qiān) = 2,000
- 五千 (wǔ qiān) = 5,000

For numbers like 1,500, combine: 一千五百 (yī qiān wǔ bǎi).

For numbers like 2,050, use 零: 二千零五十 (èr qiān líng wǔ shí).

content.title: Numbers 100-999 with 百 (bǎi)
content.body:
百 (bǎi) means "hundred" in Chinese. To form hundreds, place a digit (1-9) before 百:

- 一百 (yī bǎi) = 100
- 二百 (èr bǎi) = 200
- 三百 (sān bǎi) = 300
- 九百 (jiǔ bǎi) = 900

For numbers like 150, combine 百 with tens and ones: 一百五十 (yī bǎi wǔ shí).

For numbers like 205, use 零 (líng) for the zero placehold

content.title: Zero placeholder in thousands
content.body:
Use 零 (líng) for missing place values in thousands:

- 一千零一 (yī qiān líng yī) = 1,001 (no hundreds, no tens)
- 二千零五十 (èr qiān líng wǔ shí) = 2,050 (no hundreds)
- 三千零八 (sān qiān líng bā) = 3,008 (no hundreds, no tens)

But: 一千五百 (yī qiān wǔ bǎi) = 1,500 (no 零 because hundreds are present).

content.title: Currency: 元 (yuán) and 块 (kuài)
content.body:
The official Chinese currency unit is 元 (yuán), also called 块 (kuài) in spoken Chinese:

- 五元 (wǔ yuán) = 5 yuan (formal/written)
- 五块 (wǔ kuài) = 5 yuan (spoken/informal)

Both mean the same thing. 块 is more common in daily conversation.

content.title: Price comparison and calculation
content.body:
You're shopping for three books:
- Book 1: 四十五块八 (sì shí wǔ kuài bā)
- Book 2: 八十三元 (bā shí sān yuán)
- Book 3: 一百二十块五 (yī bǎi èr shí kuài wǔ)

content.title: Real shopping scenario
content.body:
You're at a market in Beijing. You see three items:
- Item A: 一百八十五块 (yī bǎi bā shí wǔ kuài)
- Item B: 三百二十元 (sān bǎi èr shí yuán)
- Item C: 六百五 (liù bǎi wǔ)

content.title: Complex prices with decimals
content.body:
Combine yuan, jiǎo/máo, and sometimes fēn for precise prices:

- 一百二十五块八毛 (yī bǎi èr shí wǔ kuài bā máo) = 125.8 yuan
- 三百零五元五角 (sān bǎi líng wǔ yuán wǔ jiǎo) = 305.5 yuan
- 两千块零三毛 (liǎng qiān kuài líng sān máo) = 2,000.3 yuan

Note: When there are no full tens/hundreds, 零 can still appear.

content.title: Shortened price expressions
content.body:
In spoken Chinese, prices are often shortened:

- 三块五 (sān kuài wǔ) = 3.5 yuan (毛 omitted)
- 十块八 (shí kuài bā) = 10.8 yuan (毛 omitted)
- 两百五 (liǎng bǎi wǔ) = 250 yuan (十 omitted from 五十)

This is informal but very common in everyday shopping. Note: 两百五 is ambiguous — while it most commonly means 250

content.title: Expressing prices with yuan and jiǎo
content.body:
To express prices with both yuan and jiǎo/máo:

- 三块五毛 (sān kuài wǔ máo) = 3.5 yuan
- 十元八角 (shí yuán bā jiǎo) = 10.8 yuan
- 二十块七毛 (èrshí kuài qī máo) = 20.7 yuan

You can also shorten it: 三块五 (sān kuài wǔ) = 3.5 yuan (the 毛 is implied).

content.title: Zero placeholder 零 in hundreds
content.body:
When there's a gap in place values (like 101, 205, 408), use 零 (líng) as a placeholder:

- 一百零一 (yī bǎi líng yī) = 101
- 三百零五 (sān bǎi líng wǔ) = 305
- 六百零八 (liù bǎi líng bā) = 608

You don't use 零 when the tens place has a value: 一百五十 (yī bǎi wǔ shí) = 150 (no 零 needed).

content.title: Asking prices: 多少钱 (duōshao qián)
content.body:
多少钱？(duōshao qián?) = "How much money?" / "How much does it cost?"

This is the standard way to ask about prices:
- 多少钱？(duōshao qián?) = How much?
- 这个多少钱？(zhège duōshao qián?) = How much is this?

content.title: Number comprehension with context
content.body:
Context helps determine whether numbers refer to prices, quantities, or other measurements:

- 五百学生 (wǔ bǎi xuésheng) = 500 students
- 五百块 (wǔ bǎi kuài) = 500 yuan
- 一万人 (yī wàn rén) = 10,000 people
- 一万元 (yī wàn yuán) = 10,000 yuan

content.title: Reading larger prices
content.body:
Combine number formation with currency units for larger prices:

- 一百五十块 (yī bǎi wǔ shí kuài) = 150 yuan
- 三百八十五元 (sān bǎi bā shí wǔ yuán) = 385 yuan
- 一千二百块 (yī qiān èr bǎi kuài) = 1,200 yuan
- 五千六百元 (wǔ qiān liù bǎi yuán) = 5,600 yuan

content.title: Shopping dialogue structure
content.body:
A typical price inquiry follows this pattern:

Customer: 这个多少钱？(zhège duōshao qián?) = How much is this?
Seller: 五十块。(wǔ shí kuài.) = 50 yuan.

Or with more detail:
Customer: 那个多少钱？(nàge duōshao qián?)
Seller: 一百二十五块五毛。(yī bǎi èr shí wǔ kuài wǔ máo.)

content.title: Large number reading in context
content.body:
You see these statistics about a Chinese city:
- Population: 五百万 (wǔ bǎi wàn)
- Annual visitors: 三千万 (sān qiān wàn)
- Average apartment price: 两百五十万元 (liǎng bǎi wǔ shí wàn yuán)

content.title: Asking prices: 这个多少钱 (zhège duōshao qián)
content.body:
这个 (zhège) means "this one" or "this." Combine it with 多少钱？to ask about specific items:

- 这个多少钱？(zhège duōshao qián?) = How much is this?
- 那个多少钱？(nàge duōshao qián?) = How much is that?

这个 points to things near you; 那个 points to things farther away.

content.title: Ten thousand: 万 (wàn)
content.body:
Chinese counts in units of ten thousand. 万 (wàn) = 10,000:

- 一万 (yī wàn) = 10,000
- 二万 (èr wàn) = 20,000
- 十万 (shí wàn) = 100,000

Unlike English which groups by thousands, Chinese groups by 万 (ten thousands).
